name: Castillo de Peracense & Molinos de Viento de Ojos Negros - circular depression of Jiloca
name_original: Castillo de Peracense & Molinos de Viento de Ojos Negros - circular depresión del Jiloca
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Monreal del Campo traverse a diverse landscape where the Iberian Mountain Range meets the Ebro River basin. The region features a mix of fertile valleys, open plains, and reforested pine forests, providing varied terrain for cyclists. Natural features like Los Ojos de Monreal, the source of the Jiloca River, contribute to the area's distinct character. Quiet minor roads offer a suitable environment for road cycling. Best road cycling routes around Monreal del Campo The most popular road cycling route is Castillo de Peracense & Molinos de Viento de Ojos Negros - circular depression of Jiloca, a 40.0 miles (64.4 km) trail that takes 3 hours 0 minutes to complete. This moderate route passes historical landmarks and explores the Jiloca depression. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Las Tres Taifas Trail, a moderate 56.0 miles (90.1 km) path. This route offers varied terrain and is part of the broader Camino del Cid network. Local road cyclists also love the Ojos Negros Mill – Ojos Negros Greenway loop from Monreal del Campo, a 54.6 miles (87.9 km) trail leading through historical sites and greenway sections, often completed in about 3 hours 58 minutes. Road cycling around Monreal del Campo is defined by its varied terrain, including the Jiloca River valley, extensive pine forests, and the transition zones of the Iberian Mountain Range. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to more challenging routes. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 50 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Monreal del Campo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Monreal del Campo?
A: Komoot lists over 10 road cycling routes around Monreal del Campo, offering a variety of distances and difficulties to explore the region's diverse landscapes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Monreal del Campo?
A: The terrain around Monreal del Campo is quite varied, featuring a mix of fertile valleys, open plains, and reforested pine forests. You'll find sections suitable for speed, as well as climbs that test endurance, particularly where the Iberian Mountain Range meets the Ebro River basin. Many routes utilize quiet minor roads, enhancing the cycling experience.

Q: Are there any historical or natural landmarks I can see while cycling?
A: Yes, many routes pass by significant points of interest. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e968462022/castillo-de-peracense-molinos-de-viento-de-ojos-negros-circular-depression-of-jiloca" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de Peracense & Molinos de Viento de Ojos Negros - circular depression of Jiloca&lt;/a&gt; route explores historical landmarks and the Jiloca depression. You might also encounter the unique natural phenomenon of Los Ojos de Monreal, the source of the Jiloca River, or the extensive reforested pine forests.

Q: Can I find road cycling routes suitable for different fitness levels?
A: Yes, the area caters to various abilities. While there are no easy routes listed, you'll find 7 moderate routes and 4 difficult routes. This allows cyclists to choose paths that match their endurance and experience, from steady rides through valleys to more challenging climbs in the transition zones of the Iberian Mountain Range.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, several popular routes are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e968462022/castillo-de-peracense-molinos-de-viento-de-ojos-negros-circular-depression-of-jiloca" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de Peracense & Molinos de Viento de Ojos Negros - circular depression of Jiloca&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/5317625" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojos Negros Mill – Ojos Negros Greenway loop from Monreal del Campo&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20142962" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bello – Monreal del Campo loop from Monreal del Campo&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best season for road cycling in Monreal del Campo?
A: Monreal del Campo is generally pleasant for cycling during spring and autumn. These seasons offer milder temperatures, making longer rides more comfortable. The varied landscapes, including pine forests and river valleys, are particularly scenic during these times.

Q: Is Monreal del Campo part of the Camino del Cid, and can I cycle sections of it?
A: Yes, Monreal del Campo is a key point along the renowned Camino del Cid, a historical and literary route popular with cyclists. Routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e963858964/las-tres-taifas-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Las Tres Taifas Trail&lt;/a&gt; are part of this broader network, allowing you to experience its cultural and historical legacy on two wheels.

Q: What unique natural features should I look out for on a ride?
A: Keep an eye out for Los Ojos de Monreal, a significant wetland complex formed by interconnected underground springs, which is the natural source of the Jiloca River. The region also boasts over 600 hectares of reforested pine forests, offering scenic sections and a chance to spot local wildlife.

Q: Are there any attractions in Monreal del Campo itself that I can visit after a ride?
A: Absolutely! In Monreal del Campo, you can visit the Saffron Museum (Museo del Azafrán) in the Plaza Mayor, which offers an interactive experience about the region's historically important saffron cultivation. The town also features older buildings and palace houses, reflecting its 12th-century origins.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by the Ojos Negros Mill?
A: Yes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/5317625" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojos Negros Mill – Ojos Negros Greenway loop from Monreal del Campo&lt;/a&gt; specifically leads through historical sites including the restored 16th-17th-century &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4852508" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojos Negros Mill&lt;/a&gt;, offering insight into local heritage.

Q: What makes road cycling in Monreal del Campo unique?
A: Road cycling in Monreal del Campo stands out due to its unique geographical position where the Iberian Mountain Range meets the Ebro River basin, creating a landscape of contrasts. This results in diverse terrain, from the fertile Jiloca River valley to extensive pine forests and open plains, all accessible via quiet minor roads. The blend of natural beauty and historical significance, including its connection to the Camino del Cid, offers a rich and varied cycling experience.

Q: Can I combine road cycling with exploring the local pine forests?
A: Yes, the region around Monreal del Campo features over 600 hectares of reforested pine forests. Many cycling routes traverse or run alongside these forests, allowing you to enjoy the scenic paths and the natural environment. These areas are home to various pine species, oaks, and savin junipers, and provide a habitat for wildlife.

Q: Where can I find parking for my car when starting a cycling route in Monreal del Campo?
A: Monreal del Campo, as a smaller town, generally offers public parking options within the town center or near key attractions. While specific cycling parking areas are not detailed, you should be able to find suitable spots to park your car before embarking on your ride.
tips:
Peracense Castle has an exceptional history spanning several eras and societies. This impressive castle, built in the 13th century, took advantage of the natural conditions of the terrain and played a key role in the defence of the Kingdom of Aragon during the wars with Castile. A fascinating visit to the past! 🏰
I hope you enjoy your visit to Peracense Castle! 😊🏰

An icon of the town of Ojos Negros, this mill dates back to the 16th-17th century and today can be seen fully restored. You can enter it and inside see its different rooms that will allow you to understand how this wonderful work of engineering worked. The warehouse is located on the ground floor and all the machinery was located above it.

Monreal del Campo is a fascinating place where you can learn all about the cultivation and harvesting of saffron. Located in the Plaza Mayor, the museum offers an interactive experience with traditional tools, historical photographs and workshops for the whole family.🌺
